title: Regression: Large file attachments (PDF, possibly images) corrupted during upload — binary encoding issue suspected
problem: PDF attachments uploaded to tasks are corrupted and cannot be opened. The issue is a regression from approximately 1-2 weeks ago. It primarily affects larger, multi-page PDFs (reports with charts), while small single-page PDFs appear unaffected. Downloaded files retain approximately the same file size as the originals but the content is damaged. There is an unconfirmed report of an image also appearing corrupted after upload, suggesting the issue may affect the general file upload pipeline rather than being PDF-specific.
root_cause_hypothesis: A recent change in the file upload or storage pipeline is corrupting binary file data. The pattern — file size preserved, content damaged, size-dependent, likely affecting multiple file types — is consistent with binary data being processed as text (e.g., a new middleware applying UTF-8 encoding, a streaming change that corrupts byte sequences, or a chunked upload change that mishandles binary boundaries). Investigate changes to the upload pipeline, storage layer, or any middleware/proxy in the request path from the past two weeks.
reproduction_steps: Prepare a multi-page PDF file with embedded charts or images (a simple report-style document, likely >1-2 MB)
Upload the PDF as an attachment to any task in TaskFlow
Download the attached file from the task
Attempt to open the downloaded PDF — it should report as damaged/corrupted
Repeat with a small single-page PDF to confirm it uploads correctly (control case)
environment: Not browser- or OS-specific based on the report (multiple customers affected across their workflow). Server-side regression.
impact: Multiple customers are blocked from using file attachments for larger documents. The reporter's team relies heavily on this feature and has customers waiting. Core workflow disruption, not just a cosmetic issue.
recommended_fix: 1. Review all commits to the file upload pipeline, storage service, and any related middleware/proxies from the past two weeks. 2. Look specifically for changes involving: stream handling, encoding/charset processing, multipart form parsing, chunked transfer encoding, or binary vs. text mode file operations. 3. Perform a binary diff between an original PDF and the corrupted downloaded version to identify the corruption pattern (e.g., byte substitution at encoding boundaries, null byte insertion, truncation at chunk boundaries). 4. Check if any infrastructure changes (reverse proxy config, CDN, API gateway) were deployed in the same timeframe.
proposed_test_case: Upload files of varying sizes (100KB, 1MB, 5MB, 10MB) and types (PDF, PNG, DOCX) through the attachment pipeline. For each, perform a byte-for-byte comparison (SHA-256 hash) between the original and downloaded file. All hashes must match. Add this as an integration test against the upload/download round-trip.
information_gaps: Exact file size threshold where corruption begins (reporter could not specify, but testing with graduated sizes will reveal this)
Whether non-PDF file types are also affected (one anecdotal report of a corrupted image — reproduction testing should cover multiple types)
Exact error message from the PDF viewer (reporter said 'damaged or corrupted' which is sufficient for triage)
Specific git commits or deployments in the relevant timeframe (requires codebase access, not reporter knowledge)
